nodes.text: Hi, good evening. Frank Delaney. I know how it looks, I promise. Before you pull it up — let me just explain, because I've had to explain it a few times today.

responses.text: I'll pull it up first and we can go from there.

nodes.text: My partner usually handles my personal prescriptions. We share a practice. He's been in Vancouver for two weeks — family thing. I had a back flare-up three days ago, nothing serious, and I wrote myself a script for the hydrocodone I've been on before. I know. I know it's not ideal. But it's all documented — my chart is at the practice, my history with this medication is documented. I'm not some stranger.

responses.text: I appreciate you explaining. I still need to run your ID.
I understand the situation. What's the prescriber NPI on the form?

nodes.text: Of course, of course. Here's the ID. I just want to flag it upfront so it's not a surprise — the prescriber and the patient are the same person. I'm a GP. I've been in practice here nineteen years.

responses.text: I see that. I need to verify this.

nodes.text: Yes, here. It's my hospital ID — no wait, I have the driver's license. Here. The white coat is mine, I just didn't want to carry it in. Old habit.

Nine nine zero zero one one two two three three. Mine. It should show as active — I'm still seeing patients. This is really just a documentation issue more than anything else. It's a gap in how we handle vacation coverage and I've been meaning to address it.

responses.text: Your NPI is valid. That's not the issue.

nodes.text: The prescription is clean. Everything is documented. I have been prescribing for nearly two decades and I have never done anything that wasn't documented. I just need the medication.

responses.text: I need to flag this for review. A physician cannot prescribe to themselves.

nodes.text: I understand your position. I do. I'm just asking you to use your judgment here. I'm a doctor. I know what I need.
